reasoning.typography: Strong mono/grotesque pairing (Berkeley Mono + Cursor Gothic). The mono feels purposeful for a code editor brand, but the execution isn't radical enough to be a 9+.
reasoning.hierarchy: Hero headline dominates well, product screenshot gets proper prominence. But the social proof logo grid is uniform/weak — no clear visual anchor beyond the screenshot itself.
reasoning.distinctiveness: Dark theme + code editor UI is category-appropriate but not unique. The headline 'extraordinarily productive' could swap for any productivity tool. Product screenshot does heavy lifting, copy doesn't.
reasoning.color: Restrained black/white/orange accent system. Orange used sparingly (~3-5% on CTAs). Clean discipline, though dark-on-dark feels safe rather than bold.
reasoning.density: Confident whitespace around hero elements. Product screenshot breathes. Logo grid has good rhythm. Not generic padding, but not pushing boundaries either.
reasoning.voice: Generic SaaS IA: headline → CTA → product shot → social proof logos. No story arc, no opinionated structure. 'Trusted every day' is stencil language. Doesn't reflect how developers actually think about tools.

winning_patterns: Monospace typography choice signals technical authenticity
Product-first — massive screenshot shows actual interface, not metaphors
Restrained color palette with disciplined accent usage
Logo grid using actual recognizable brands (Stripe, OpenAI, Adobe) not generic icons
anti_patterns: Hero headline is productivity-tool Mad Libs: '[adjective] productive'
'Trusted every day by teams that build world-class software' — pure stencil copy
Zero information architecture personality — straight-line conversion funnel
Dark theme feels expected for dev tools, not a choice with POV
No differentiation from other AI code assistants beyond branding
_note: AI code editor — direct Zerg adjacency
